books.title: The Old Man and the Sea
books.author: Ernest Hemingway
books.context: I came to this after abandoning Henry James at chapter 15. The Portrait of a Lady left me cold — characters hooked to their ideas, nobody real, nobody moving. I needed a calibration reset. A man alone with his craft. No noise. This is what I found.

themes.title: Age as clarification
themes.body: I keep returning to what gets stripped away with age and what stays. Pride goes first — it becomes a luxury I can no longer afford. Humility is not weakness; it is the actual legs I walk on now. When I read about the old man's eyes — unchanged, cheerful, undefeated — I felt seen. The soul holds its life better than the flesh does.

themes.title: Memory and distillation
themes.body: I question my own memories the older I get. Did I remember that, or did someone tell me? But I have come to think accuracy is not really the point. What matters is what gets distilled — what remains after the rest falls away. That is what memoir is, for me. Not the record. The residue.

themes.title: Dreams and ownership
themes.body: The old man no longer dreams of storms or women or great fish. He dreams of places. I read that and thought: this is what maturity looks like in the interior life. No more wanting things that can be owned. The bigger themes — those can never be owned, only shared.

themes.title: The Swiss knife of age
themes.body: I made a list of what the old man actually has: luck, humility, experience, trickery, mysticism, resolution, empathy, sympathy, and lions in dreams. That is not a catalogue of losses. That is a toolkit. I recognise most of those things in myself now, and I did not have them when I was young.

themes.title: The big fish
themes.body: Would he go out at all if there were no fish to hope for? I did not answer that when I read it. I still have not answered it. It is the question underneath everything — about what keeps a person moving, what faith actually is when you strip the word down.

entries.quote: Everything about him was old except his eyes and they were the same color as the sea and were cheerful and undefeated.
entries.response: And the eyes are the doors to the soul, it changes and matures but it holds its life better than the flesh..

entries.quote: Can you really remember that or did I just tell it to you? / I remember everything from when we first went together.
entries.response: The younger you are, the more sure about you memories. The older I become I question the memory from the memoirs.. but does it matter?! Not really, it's what is distilled that matters..

entries.quote: Thank you, the old man said. He was too simple to wonder when he had attained humility. But he knew he had attained it and he knew it was not disgraceful...
entries.response: Wow.. how true.. Pride is a luxury for the young, humility the legs of aging.. life do nibble on pride, a survival mechanism is it?!

entries.quote: He no longer dreamed of storms, nor of women, nor of great occurrences, nor of great fish, nor fights, nor contests of strength, nor of his wife. He only dreamed of places...
entries.response: Of man and his dreams.. no more for wants and ownerships.. it's dreams of the bigger themes that can never be owned, but shared.

entries.quote: My big fish must be somewhere.
entries.response: The hope of a big fish, would he go out if it's none..

entries.quote: I am a strange old man. / But are you strong enough now for a truly big fish? / I think so. And there are many tricks.
entries.response: Let's count the Swiss knife tools of the aged as Hemingway thinks; luck, humility, experience, trickery, mysticism, resolution, empathy, sympathy and lions in dreams.

reflection.text:
I'm starting to see Santiago not as some tragic figure, but as someone who's figured out something I'm still wrestling with. When I first read about his eyes being "cheerful and undefeated," I thought it was just poetic description. But now, six passages in, I realize Hemingway is showing me what survives when everything else gets stripped away. Santiago has lost his strength, his luck, his youth - hell, even the kid can't fish with him anymore - but there's this core that remains untouched. It's not stubbornness; it's something deeper.

What's catching me off guard is how Santiago's relationship with memory and dreams has evolved. He doesn't question whether his memories are real anymore, and his dreams have shifted from wanting things to simply being in places. There's something profoundly liberating about that transition that I didn't expect to find moving. I keep thinking about my own dreams lately - am I still dreaming of contests and acquisitions, or am I starting to dream of spaces, of being rather than having?

The humility passage hit me hardest. "Too simple to wonder when he had attained humility" - that phrase keeps rolling around in my head. I've always thought of humility as something you work toward, analyze, achieve consciously. But Santiago just inhabits it naturally now, without the self-consciousness that would make it performative. It makes me wonder if I'm overthinking my own growth, if real wisdom comes from this kind of unconscious settling into what you've become.

And yet he still believes in his big fish. That's what's keeping me hooked - this isn't a story about resignation or quiet defeat. Santiago has all these tools now, this "Swiss knife of the aged" as I put it, but he's still out there hunting for something magnificent. The combination of deep acceptance and persistent hope feels like a paradox I need to understand better. Maybe that's what undefeated eyes actually look like.

books.title: The Heart's Invisible Furies
books.author: John Boyne
books.context: Recommended to me as East of Eden crossed with Covenant of Water — modern, sharper, faster. Life across decades. Identity, family, exile, belonging. I started it after Hemingway.

entries.quote: The Good People of Goleen Long before we discovered that he had fathered two children by two different women, one in Drimoleague and one in Cl...
entries.response: I think this has to count as one of the most memorable first paragraph of a novel .. it starts with a sarcastic title of a chapter , followed by the act of a sinful priest and ends with a mother called a whore!. The Good People of Goleen - capitalised-but no one seems to be Good.

entries.quote: "Good luck to you now." And with that she took her leave of Goleen, the place of her birth, which she would not see again for more than sixty years, whe...
entries.response: The ending paragraph, tells us with what to come .. the cast away will return and probably nothing would have changed in the village but for the occupied graveyard housing the people who shunned her 60 years earlier. The whole chapter feels more comic than tragic, the humour echoes the style of Frank McCourt in Angela's ashes.

From Out Near Ballincollig
You keep about the meaning of this title until you half way in this chapter when Sean McIntyre introduces himself as bad lad from out near Ballincollig, somewhere in Cork County where it gets to your head if you live there long enough. The chapter takes the two youngsters fleeing from their own miseries for freedom that is yet to materialise. Both share tender exchange of their misfortune as they head to the Big Smoke in Dublin. The bus ride is their transition from one life to another and it is a nice way of introducing the two characters to us.
